title: Double-ended PQ
description: Implement DEPQ with min & max extraction

examples.input: Insert 5, Insert 3, Insert 8, deleteMin, deleteMax, Insert 1, deleteMin
examples.explanation: Step 1: Insert 5 with ID 1 → min-heap: [(5,1)], max-heap: [(-5,1)], active: {1:5}. Step 2: Insert 3 with ID 2 → min-heap: [(3,2),(5,1)], max-heap: [(-5,1),(-3,2)], active: {1:5,2:3}. Step 3: Insert 8 with ID 3 → min-heap: [(3,2),(5,1),(8,3)], max-heap: [(-8,3),(-5,1),(-3,2)], active: {1:5,2:3,3:8}. Step 4: deleteMin → min-heap top (3,2) is active, pop it, mark ID 2 inactive, rebalance not needed (sizes: min-heap 2, max-heap 3), return 3. Step 5: deleteMax → max-heap top (-8,3) is active, pop it, mark ID 3 inactive, rebalance not needed (sizes: min-heap 2, max-heap 2), return 8. Step 6: Insert 1 with ID 4 → min-heap: [(1,4),(5,1)], max-heap: [(-5,1),(-1,4)], active: {1:5,4:1}. Step 7: deleteMin → min-heap top (1,4) is active, pop it, mark ID 4 inactive, return 1.

examples.input: Insert 10, Insert 20, deleteMin, Insert 5, deleteMax, deleteMin
examples.explanation: Step 1: Insert 10 with ID 1 → min-heap: [(10,1)], max-heap: [(-10,1)], active: {1:10}. Step 2: Insert 20 with ID 2 → min-heap: [(10,1),(20,2)], max-heap: [(-20,2),(-10,1)], active: {1:10,2:20}. Step 3: deleteMin → min-heap top (10,1) active, pop it, mark ID 1 inactive, rebalance not needed, return 10. Step 4: Insert 5 with ID 3 → min-heap: [(5,3),(20,2)], max-heap: [(-20,2),(-5,3)], active: {2:20,3:5}. Step 5: deleteMax → max-heap top (-20,2) active, pop it, mark ID 2 inactive, rebalance not needed, return 20. Step 6: deleteMin → min-heap top (5,3) active, pop it, return 5.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Two Heaps with Balancing
approaches.summary: Use a min-heap and a max-heap to store elements, with balancing to ensure both heaps contain roughly half the elements for efficient min and max extraction. This approach leverages standard heap operations but requires careful synchronization between the two heaps.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n) for insert, deleteMin, and deleteMax

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Double-Ended Priority Queue with Two Heaps
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of DEPQ using min-heap and max-heap with balancing mechanism.
ladders.explanation: A Double-Ended Priority Queue (DEPQ) requires efficient extraction of both minimum and maximum elements. The two-heap approach maintains a min-heap for minimum extraction and a max-heap for maximum extraction, storing all elements in both heaps. The key challenge is keeping both heaps synchronized so they contain the same elements. We solve this by storing each element with a unique identifier (like an index or timestamp) and using a balancing mechanism to ensure both heaps contain roughly half the elements. For insertion, we add the element to both heaps with its identifier. For deleteMin, we extract from min-heap, mark the element as deleted in the max-heap using the identifier, and rebalance if needed. For deleteMax, we do the symmetric operation. The balancing ensures neither heap grows too large, maintaining O(log n) operations. Intuition: By maintaining two complementary views of the same data, we get efficient access to both extremes while keeping updates logarithmic through lazy deletion and periodic rebalancing. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize min-heap and max-heap, plus a dictionary to track active elements by ID. 2. Insert: Generate unique ID, push (value, ID) to both heaps, store ID as active. 3. DeleteMin: While min-heap top has inactive ID (checked via dictionary), pop it. Then pop (value, ID), mark ID inactive, rebalance if min-heap size > max-heap size + 1 by moving elements. 4. DeleteMax: Symmetric to deleteMin. 5. Rebalance: Transfer elements between heaps to maintain size difference ≤ 1. Time: O(log n) per operation due to heap operations and amortized rebalancing. Space: O(n) for heaps and dictionary. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into a working DEPQ. Common mistakes: Forgetting to clean inactive elements from heap tops, incorrect rebalancing logic, or not handling empty queue. Real code pattern: Use heapq for min-heap and negated values for max-heap in Python.

examples.input: Insert 5, Insert 3, Insert 8, deleteMin, deleteMax, Insert 1, deleteMin
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 5 with ID 1 → min-heap: [(5,1)], max-heap: [(-5,1)], active: {1:5}. Step 2: Insert 3 with ID 2 → min-heap: [(3,2),(5,1)], max-heap: [(-5,1),(-3,2)], active: {1:5,2:3}. Step 3: Insert 8 with ID 3 → min-heap: [(3,2),(5,1),(8,3)], max-heap: [(-8,3),(-5,1),(-3,2)], active: {1:5,2:3,3:8}. Step 4: deleteMin → min-heap top (3,2) is active, pop it, mark ID 2 inactive, rebalance not needed (sizes: min-heap 2, max-heap 3), return 3. Step 5: deleteMax → max-heap top (-8,3) is active, pop it, mark ID 3 inactive, rebalance not needed (sizes: min-heap 2, max-heap 2), return 8. Step 6: Insert 1 with ID 4 → min-heap: [(1,4),(5,1)], max-heap: [(-5,1),(-1,4)], active: {1:5,4:1}. Step 7: deleteMin → min-heap top (1,4) is active, pop it, mark ID 4 inactive, return 1.

examples.input: Insert 10, Insert 20, deleteMin, Insert 5, deleteMax, deleteMin
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 10 with ID 1 → min-heap: [(10,1)], max-heap: [(-10,1)], active: {1:10}. Step 2: Insert 20 with ID 2 → min-heap: [(10,1),(20,2)], max-heap: [(-20,2),(-10,1)], active: {1:10,2:20}. Step 3: deleteMin → min-heap top (10,1) active, pop it, mark ID 1 inactive, rebalance not needed, return 10. Step 4: Insert 5 with ID 3 → min-heap: [(5,3),(20,2)], max-heap: [(-20,2),(-5,3)], active: {2:20,3:5}. Step 5: deleteMax → max-heap top (-20,2) active, pop it, mark ID 2 inactive, rebalance not needed, return 20. Step 6: deleteMin → min-heap top (5,3) active, pop it, return 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Balancing Two Heaps After Deletion
ladders.desc: Implement rebalancing to maintain size difference ≤ 1 between min-heap and max-heap.
ladders.explanation: After each deletion (deleteMin or deleteMax), the two heaps may become unbalanced, with one heap significantly larger than the other. This imbalance can degrade performance because future operations might require moving many elements. The rebalancing subroutine ensures that the size difference between the min-heap and max-heap is at most 1, keeping operations efficient. Intuition: By keeping both heaps roughly equal in size, we minimize the cost of transferring elements during deletions, maintaining amortized O(log n) time. The algorithm: 1. Compare sizes of min-heap and max-heap. 2. If |size(min-heap) - size(max-heap)| > 1, identify the larger heap. 3. While the larger heap has more elements, pop its top, check if the element's ID is still active (using a dictionary), and if active, push it to the smaller heap. If inactive, discard it and continue popping until an active element is found. 4. Repeat until size difference ≤ 1. Time: O(k log n) where k is number of elements moved, but amortized O(log n) due to infrequent rebalancing. Space: O(1) extra. Connection: This subroutine is called after deletions in L0 to maintain heap balance. Common mistakes: Not checking active status during transfer, causing inactive elements to be moved, or infinite loops if not discarding inactive elements. Real code pattern: Use while loop with size comparison and heap pops/pushes.

examples.input: Min-heap size: 5, Max-heap size: 2, active IDs: {1,2,3,4,5} in min-heap, {6,7} in max-heap. Rebalance.
examples.trace: Step 1: Size difference = |5-2| = 3 > 1, min-heap is larger. Step 2: Pop top from min-heap (smallest element, say value 10 with ID 1). ID 1 is active, so push (10,1) to max-heap (as -10). Now sizes: min-heap 4, max-heap 3. Step 3: Difference = |4-3| = 1 ≤ 1, stop. Elements moved: 1.

examples.input: Min-heap size: 1, Max-heap size: 4, active IDs: {1} in min-heap, {2,3,4,5} in max-heap. Rebalance.
examples.trace: Step 1: Size difference = |1-4| = 3 > 1, max-heap is larger. Step 2: Pop top from max-heap (largest element, say value 20 with ID 2). ID 2 is active, push (20,2) to min-heap. Sizes: min-heap 2, max-heap 3. Step 3: Difference = 1, but max-heap still larger? Wait, recalc: |2-3| = 1 ≤ 1, so stop. Actually, let's continue: After first move, sizes: min-heap 2, max-heap 3, difference 1, but we need to check if balanced? The condition is ≤1, so it's balanced. No further moves. Output sizes: min-heap 2, max-heap 3. Correction: The output should be min-heap 2, max-heap 3, as difference is 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Lazy Deletion with Identifier Tracking
ladders.desc: Use unique identifiers to mark deleted elements in the opposite heap without immediate removal.
ladders.explanation: In the two-heap DEPQ, when we delete an element from one heap (e.g., deleteMin from min-heap), it must also be removed from the other heap (max-heap) to keep heaps synchronized. Immediate removal from the other heap would require O(n) search. Lazy deletion solves this by assigning each element a unique identifier (ID) and maintaining a dictionary of active IDs. When an element is deleted from one heap, we mark its ID as inactive in the dictionary. Later, when accessing the other heap, we check if the top element's ID is active; if not, we pop it and discard it. This defers the actual removal until necessary, keeping operations O(log n). Intuition: Instead of costly searches, we use IDs as 'tombstones' to invalidate elements efficiently. Step-by-step: 1. On insertion, generate a unique ID (e.g., incrementing counter), store element with ID in both heaps, and add ID to active dictionary. 2. On deletion from one heap, pop the top, get its ID, mark ID as inactive in dictionary. 3. Before any heap operation that uses the top (e.g., for rebalancing or next deletion), while the top's ID is inactive, pop and discard it. Time: O(log n) for heap pops, amortized over operations. Space: O(n) for dictionary. Connection: This logic enables efficient synchronization in L1 and L0. Common mistakes: Not generating unique IDs per insertion (causing conflicts), or forgetting to check inactive IDs during rebalancing. Real code pattern: Use a counter for IDs and a set or dict for active status.

examples.input: Insert 5 (ID 1), Insert 3 (ID 2), deleteMin removes ID 2, then access max-heap top.
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 5 with ID 1 → min-heap: [(5,1)], max-heap: [(-5,1)], active: {1}. Step 2: Insert 3 with ID 2 → min-heap: [(3,2),(5,1)], max-heap: [(-5,1),(-3,2)], active: {1,2}. Step 3: deleteMin → pop (3,2) from min-heap, mark ID 2 inactive, active: {1}. Step 4: Access max-heap top → top is (-5,1), check ID 1 is active, so it's valid.

examples.input: Insert 10 (ID 1), Insert 20 (ID 2), deleteMax removes ID 2, then access min-heap top.
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 10 with ID 1 → min-heap: [(10,1)], max-heap: [(-10,1)], active: {1}. Step 2: Insert 20 with ID 2 → min-heap: [(10,1),(20,2)], max-heap: [(-20,2),(-10,1)], active: {1,2}. Step 3: deleteMax → pop (-20,2) from max-heap, mark ID 2 inactive, active: {1}. Step 4: Access min-heap top → top is (10,1), ID 1 active, valid.

ladders.title: Building Block — Implementing Max-Heap Using Min-Heap with Negation
ladders.desc: Create a max-heap by storing negated values in a min-heap.
ladders.explanation: Many programming languages provide built-in min-heap implementations (e.g., heapq in Python) but not max-heaps. We can simulate a max-heap using a min-heap by negating all values before insertion and negating back after extraction. Intuition: A max-heap returns the largest element, but a min-heap returns the smallest. By negating values, the largest positive becomes the smallest negative, so the min-heap effectively returns the max when we negate the result. For example, values 5 and 10: negated to -5 and -10; min-heap returns -10, which when negated back gives 10, the max. Step-by-step: 1. For insertion into max-heap, push -value into the min-heap. 2. For extraction from max-heap, pop from min-heap to get -value, then return -(-value) = value. 3. Ensure to handle other data (like IDs) alongside values. Time: O(log n) same as min-heap operations. Space: O(n). Connection: This building block is used in L2 and above to maintain the max-heap in the two-heap DEPQ. Common mistakes: Forgetting to negate during insertion or extraction, or mishandling when values are negative (negation flips sign, but algorithm still works as it preserves order). Real code pattern: Use heapq.heappush(max_heap, (-value, id)) and heapq.heappop(max_heap) returning (-value, id).

examples.input: Insert 7, Insert 3 into max-heap, then extract max.
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 7 → push -7 into min-heap: [-7]. Step 2: Insert 3 → push -3: [-7,-3] (min-heap order: -7 < -3, so -7 is top). Step 3: Extract max → pop min-heap top -7, negate: -(-7) = 7, return 7.

examples.input: Insert -5, Insert -1 into max-heap, then extract max.
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert -5 → push -(-5)=5 into min-heap: [5]. Step 2: Insert -1 → push -(-1)=1: [1,5] (min-heap top is 1). Step 3: Extract max → pop 1, negate: -1, return -1 (since -1 > -5).

testCases.input: Insert 1, extract max, insert 2, extract max
Insert 9, Insert 1, Insert 5, extract max, extract max

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Insert and Extract from a Min-Heap
ladders.desc: Perform fundamental heap operations: insert an element and extract the minimum.
ladders.explanation: A min-heap is a complete binary tree where each node is smaller than or equal to its children, ensuring the minimum element is at the root. The basic operations are insert (add element) and extractMin (remove and return the minimum). Intuition: Heaps maintain partial order to allow efficient access to extremes. Insertion adds the element at the end and 'bubbles up' by swapping with parent until heap property is restored. Extraction removes the root, replaces it with the last element, and 'bubbles down' by swapping with the smaller child until heap property is restored. Step-by-step for insert: 1. Add element to end of array. 2. Compare with parent; if smaller, swap. 3. Repeat until root or parent is smaller. For extractMin: 1. Store root value. 2. Replace root with last element, remove last. 3. Compare new root with children; if larger than smaller child, swap. 4. Repeat until leaf or heap property satisfied. Time: O(log n) for both due to tree height. Space: O(1) extra per operation. Connection: This operation is used in L3 and above for managing heaps in DEPQ. Common mistakes: Not maintaining complete tree structure, incorrect comparison during bubble down, or off-by-one errors in array indices. Real code pattern: Use list for heap, with indices: parent at (i-1)//2, children at 2*i+1 and 2*i+2.

examples.input: Insert 5, Insert 3, Insert 8 into min-heap, then extractMin.
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 5 → heap: [5]. Step 2: Insert 3 → add at end: [5,3], bubble up: compare 3 with parent 5, swap → [3,5]. Step 3: Insert 8 → add: [3,5,8], bubble up: 8 > parent 5, no swap. Step 4: extractMin → root 3, replace with last 8: [8,5], bubble down: compare 8 with children 5 (only left child), 8 > 5, swap → [5,8], return 3.

examples.input: Insert 10, Insert 20, extractMin, Insert 5, extractMin.
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 10 → [10]. Step 2: Insert 20 → [10,20], bubble up: 20 > 10, no swap. Step 3: extractMin → root 10, replace with 20: [20], bubble down: no children, return 10. Step 4: Insert 5 → [20,5], bubble up: 5 < parent 20, swap → [5,20]. Step 5: extractMin → root 5, replace with 20: [20], return 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Heaps as Priority Queues
ladders.desc: Learn the basic concept of a heap data structure and its use for priority queue operations.
ladders.explanation: A heap is a specialized tree-based data structure that satisfies the heap property: in a min-heap, each parent node is less than or equal to its children, so the smallest element is at the root; in a max-heap, each parent is greater than or equal to its children, so the largest is at the root. Heaps are commonly implemented as arrays representing a complete binary tree, where for index i, parent is at floor((i-1)/2), left child at 2*i+1, and right child at 2*i+2. Heaps are used to implement priority queues, which support insert and extract-min (or extract-max) efficiently. Intuition: By keeping elements in a partially ordered tree, we can quickly access the extreme value (min or max) without sorting all elements. The tree structure ensures operations take O(log n) time because the height of a complete binary tree with n nodes is log n. This is faster than O(n) for linear search. Step-by-step: To understand heaps, visualize the tree: e.g., values [3,5,8] as min-heap with root 3, children 5 and 8. Insertion adds a new leaf and adjusts by swapping up. Extraction removes the root and adjusts by swapping down. Time: O(log n) for insert and extract. Space: O(n) to store elements. Connection: This foundation is essential for L4 and above, where heaps are used in DEPQ. Common mistakes: Confusing heaps with binary search trees (BSTs), or not recognizing the array representation. Real code pattern: In Python, heapq module provides min-heap functions.

examples.input: Min-heap with elements [2,5,7]. Insert 3.
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial heap as array: [2,5,7] representing tree: root 2, left child 5, right child 7. Step 2: Insert 3: add at end → [2,5,7,3]. Tree: new node 3 as child of 5. Step 3: Bubble up: compare 3 with parent 5, 3 < 5, so swap → [2,3,7,5]. Now tree: root 2, left child 3 (with children 7 and 5), right child none.

examples.input: Min-heap with elements [1,4,2,8]. Extract min.
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial heap: [1,4,2,8] as tree: root 1, left child 4, right child 2, and 8 as child of 4. Step 2: Extract min: remove root 1, replace with last element 8 → [8,4,2]. Step 3: Bubble down: compare new root 8 with children 4 and 2; smaller child is 2, 8 > 2, so swap → [2,4,8]. Tree: root 2, left child 4, right child 8.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Interval Heap
approaches.summary: Implement an interval heap, a specialized binary heap where each node stores a min-max pair, allowing both min and max operations in logarithmic time. This structure inherently supports double-ended operations without needing separate heaps.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n) for insert, deleteMin, and deleteMax

ladders.title: Full Problem — Complete Interval Heap Implementation
ladders.desc: Implement a double-ended priority queue using interval heap with all operations.
ladders.explanation: An interval heap is a complete binary tree where each node stores an interval [min, max] representing the minimum and maximum values in its subtree. This structure inherently supports both min and max operations efficiently. The intuition is that by maintaining min-max pairs at each node, we can propagate changes up and down the tree while preserving the heap property for both ends. The complete solution involves implementing insert, getMin, getMax, deleteMin, and deleteMax operations. For insert, we add a new element to the last position, then bubble up by comparing with parent intervals to maintain the heap property. For deleteMin, we remove the root's min, replace it with the last element's appropriate value, then bubble down by comparing with children to restore the heap. DeleteMax follows a symmetric process. The time complexity is O(log n) for all operations due to the tree height, and space is O(n) for storing elements. This feeds directly from L1's key sub-routine by combining all operations into a cohesive class. Common mistakes include incorrect interval comparisons during bubble operations or mishandling edge cases like empty heaps. Real code pattern involves a list-based heap representation with index calculations for parent and children.

examples.input: Insert 5, 3, 8, 1, 7; then deleteMin, deleteMax
examples.output: Min: 1, Max: 8, after deletions: heap maintains [3,7] at root
examples.trace: Start empty. Insert 5: heap = [[5,5]]. Insert 3: add as child, compare with parent [5,5] -> swap to get [[3,5]]. Insert 8: add as child of [3,5], compare -> becomes [[3,8],[5,5]]. Insert 1: add as child of [3,8], bubble up: compare 1 with parent min 3 -> swap, heap becomes [[1,8],[3,5],[5,5]]. Insert 7: add as child of [3,5], compare -> heap becomes [[1,8],[3,7],[5,5]]. DeleteMin: remove root min 1, replace with last element's min 5, bubble down: compare 5 with children mins 3 and 5 -> swap with left child, heap becomes [[3,8],[5,7]]. DeleteMax: remove root max 8, replace with last element's max 7, bubble down: compare 7 with children maxes 7 and ? -> no swap, heap becomes [[3,7],[5,5]].

examples.input: Insert 10, 20, 15, 5; then deleteMax, deleteMin, insert 25
examples.output: Max: 20, Min: 5, after operations: heap root [10,25]
examples.trace: Start empty. Insert 10: [[10,10]]. Insert 20: add child, compare -> [[10,20]]. Insert 15: add child of [10,20], compare -> [[10,20],[15,15]]. Insert 5: add child of [10,20], bubble up: 5 < 10 -> swap, heap becomes [[5,20],[10,15]]. DeleteMax: remove root max 20, replace with last max 15, bubble down: compare 15 with child maxes 15 and ? -> no swap, heap becomes [[5,15],[10,10]]. DeleteMin: remove root min 5, replace with last min 10, bubble down: compare 10 with child mins 10 and ? -> no swap, heap becomes [[10,15]]. Insert 25: add as child, bubble up: 25 > 15 -> swap max, heap becomes [[10,25],[15,15]].

testCases.expected: Removed: 1 and 6, heap has [2,4] at root

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Bubble Down Operation in Interval Heap
ladders.desc: Implement the bubble down (heapify) function to restore heap property after deletion.
ladders.explanation: The bubble down operation is crucial for maintaining the interval heap structure after a deletion (min or max). When an element is removed, we replace it with a value from the end of the heap, which may violate the heap property. The intuition is to recursively swap the node with its children until the interval [min, max] at each node satisfies that the min is less than or equal to the mins of its children and the max is greater than or equal to the maxes of its children. For deleteMin, we bubble down the min value by comparing it with the mins of child intervals, swapping with the smaller child min to preserve the min-heap property. For deleteMax, we bubble down the max value by comparing with child maxes, swapping with the larger child max. The algorithm checks if the current node has children, finds the appropriate child based on min or max operation, and swaps if needed, repeating until no swaps are required or a leaf is reached. Time complexity is O(log n) as it traverses the tree height, and space is O(1) for iterative implementation. This feeds into L0 by enabling efficient deletions. Common mistakes include incorrect child index calculations or not handling both min and max comparisons separately. Real code pattern uses while loops with index updates.

examples.input: Heap [[3,8],[5,7],[4,6]]; bubble down min at root after replacing min with 9
examples.trace: Start: root interval [9,8] violates min property. Compare min 9 with child mins: left child min 5, right child min 4. Smaller child min is 4 (right). Swap root min 9 with right child min 4: root becomes [4,8], right child becomes [9,6]. Now check right child [9,6]: it's a leaf, stop. Final heap: [[4,8],[5,7],[9,6]].

examples.input: Heap [[2,10],[3,9],[5,8]]; bubble down max at root after replacing max with 1
examples.trace: Start: root interval [2,1] violates max property (max should be >= child maxes). Compare max 1 with child maxes: left child max 9, right child max 8. Larger child max is 9 (left). Swap root max 1 with left child max 9: root becomes [2,9], left child becomes [3,1]. Now check left child [3,1]: it's a leaf, stop. Final heap: [[2,9],[3,1],[5,8]].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Interval Heap Structure and Properties
ladders.desc: Understand the interval heap tree structure and its min-max ordering rules.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of an interval heap lies in its tree structure and the properties that enable efficient double-ended operations. An interval heap is a complete binary tree where each node stores an interval [min, max], with min ≤ max. The key insight is that for any node, its min value is less than or equal to the min values of its children (min-heap property for the lower end), and its max value is greater than or equal to the max values of its children (max-heap property for the upper end). This dual property allows us to access both minimum and maximum elements at the root in O(1) time. The tree is typically implemented using an array, where for index i, parent is at floor((i-1)/2), left child at 2i+1, and right child at 2i+2. The intuition is that by maintaining these intervals, insertions and deletions can bubble values up or down while preserving both heap properties. This feeds into L1 by defining the rules that bubble operations must enforce. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the interval comparisons or incorrect array indexing. Real code pattern involves storing pairs in a list and defining helper functions to access mins and maxes.

examples.input: Build interval heap from elements [4,1,3,2]
examples.trace: Start with empty heap. Insert 4: root [4,4]. Insert 1: add as child, compare with parent: 1 < 4, so swap mins, root becomes [1,4]. Insert 3: add as child of [1,4], compare: 3 > 1 and 3 < 4, so interval becomes [1,4] with child [3,3]. Insert 2: add as child of [1,4], compare: 2 > 1 and 2 < 4, but need to maintain child ordering: add as second child, compare with sibling [3,3]: mins are 2 and 3, so left child becomes [2,3], right child [3,3] after adjustments. Final: root [1,4], left child [2,3], right child [3,3].

examples.input: Check heap property for heap [[2,5],[3,4],[6,6]]
examples.output: Property invalid: root max 5 is less than child max 6
examples.trace: Root interval [2,5]. Left child [3,4]: min 3 ≥ root min 2, max 4 ≤ root max 5. Right child [6,6]: min 6 ≥ root min 2, max 6 ≤ root max 5? 6 > 5, so violation! Actually, this heap is invalid because right child max 6 > root max 5. To fix, we'd need to bubble or adjust.

ladders.title: Building Block — Min-Max Pair Comparisons in a Heap
ladders.desc: Learn to compare and swap min-max pairs between nodes in a heap structure.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on how to compare and potentially swap intervals between nodes in an interval heap. Each node holds a pair [min, max], and during heap operations, we need to compare these pairs to maintain order. The intuition is that when bubbling up or down, we compare specific components: for min operations, we compare min values; for max operations, we compare max values. For example, during insertion bubble-up, if adding to a min slot, we compare the new min with the parent's min and swap if smaller; if adding to a max slot, we compare with parent's max and swap if larger. Swapping involves exchanging the entire interval or just the min/max component, depending on the operation. This is essential because it ensures the heap properties are preserved efficiently. The algorithm involves checking node indices to determine if we're at a min or max position (even indices often treat the element as min, odd as max in some implementations), then performing the appropriate comparison. Time complexity per comparison is O(1), and space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanical steps for maintaining the interval structure. Common mistakes include swapping the wrong component or not handling parity correctly. Real code pattern uses modular arithmetic to decide min/max role.

examples.input: Compare intervals [3,8] (parent) and [2,9] (child) for min bubble-up
examples.output: Swap mins because 2 < 3, resulting in [2,8] and [3,9]
examples.trace: Parent interval [3,8], child interval [2,9]. For min operation, compare mins: child min 2 vs parent min 3. Since 2 < 3, we swap the min values: parent becomes [2,8], child becomes [3,9]. Note maxes may adjust if needed, but here they stay as is because 8 and 9 are still valid.

examples.input: Compare intervals [1,5] (parent) and [2,6] (child) for max bubble-up
examples.output: Swap maxes because 6 > 5, resulting in [1,6] and [2,5]
examples.trace: Parent interval [1,5], child interval [2,6]. For max operation, compare maxes: child max 6 vs parent max 5. Since 6 > 5, we swap the max values: parent becomes [1,6], child becomes [2,5]. Mins remain as is because 1 and 2 are still ordered.

testCases.expected: No swap: child max 7 ≤ parent max 8
No swap: child min 6 ≥ parent min 5
No swap: child min 8 ≥ parent min 7
No swap: child min 2 ≥ parent min 1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Insert Element into a Binary Heap
ladders.desc: Add a new element to a binary heap and bubble up to maintain heap property.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves inserting a new element into a binary heap, which is a fundamental skill for heap data structures. The intuition is that we add the element at the end of the heap (to maintain the complete tree property), then 'bubble up' by comparing it with its parent and swapping if necessary until the heap property is restored. For a min-heap, we swap if the new element is smaller than its parent; for a max-heap, if larger. This ensures the root always holds the extreme value. The algorithm: 1) Append the element to the end of the array representing the heap. 2) Set current index to the last position. 3) While current index is not the root, compare with parent: if violation, swap and move current to parent index; else stop. Time complexity is O(log n) due to at most height swaps, and space is O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the foundational insert mechanism that extends to interval heaps. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in parent index calculation or not stopping the loop correctly. Real code pattern uses integer division for parent index.

examples.input: Insert 3 into min-heap [5,8,10]
examples.trace: Start heap: [5,8,10] (array representation). Insert 3: append to end -> [5,8,10,3]. Current index 3, parent index = floor((3-1)/2) = 1. Compare 3 with parent value 8: 3 < 8, so swap -> [5,3,10,8]. Update current to index 1. New parent index = floor((1-1)/2) = 0. Compare 3 with parent value 5: 3 < 5, swap -> [3,5,10,8]. Current at root, stop. Final heap: [3,5,10,8].

examples.input: Insert 12 into max-heap [9,5,7]
examples.trace: Start heap: [9,5,7]. Insert 12: append -> [9,5,7,12]. Current index 3, parent index = 1. Compare 12 with parent value 5: 12 > 5, swap -> [9,12,7,5]. Update current to index 1. New parent index = 0. Compare 12 with parent value 9: 12 > 9, swap -> [12,9,7,5]. Current at root, stop. Final heap: [12,9,7,5].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Heap Array Representation
ladders.desc: Understand how a binary heap is represented as an array with parent-child indexing.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers the array representation of a binary heap, which is essential for implementing heap operations efficiently. A binary heap is a complete binary tree, meaning all levels are fully filled except possibly the last, which is filled from left to right. Instead of using node objects with pointers, we can store elements in an array where the tree structure is implicit through indices. The intuition is that for any element at index i, its parent is at index floor((i-1)/2), its left child is at index 2i+1, and its right child is at index 2i+2. This allows O(1) access to parent and children, making operations like bubble-up and bubble-down fast. The array representation saves space and simplifies algorithms. For example, in a min-heap, the smallest element is at index 0. This feeds into L4 by providing the data structure basis for insert operations. Common mistakes include incorrect index calculations or not ensuring the array maintains the complete tree property. Real code pattern uses a list and helper functions for indices.

examples.input: Array [3,5,8,10,6] representing a min-heap
examples.output: Tree: root 3, children 5 and 8, grandchildren 10 and 6
examples.trace: Index 0: 3 (root). Parent of index 0: none. Left child of 0: index 1 = 5. Right child of 0: index 2 = 8. For index 1 (5): parent index floor((1-1)/2)=0, value 3; left child index 3=10; right child index 4=6. For index 2 (8): parent index 0, value 3; no children beyond array. Check heap property: 3 ≤ 5 and 3 ≤ 8; 5 ≤ 10 and 5 ≤ 6.

examples.input: Find parent and children for element at index 4 in array [9,7,5,3,1]
examples.trace: Array indices: 0:9, 1:7, 2:5, 3:3, 4:1. For index 4: parent index = floor((4-1)/2) = floor(1.5) = 1, so parent is value 7. Left child index = 2*4+1 = 9, which is beyond array length 5, so no left child. Right child index = 2*4+2 = 10, also beyond, so no children.

testCases.expected: Left child index 3 value 7, no right child

testCases.input: Array [5,8,9,10]; find left child of index 0
Array [3,6,9,12,15]; find right child of index 1

testCases.expected: Left index 1 value 2, right index 2 value 1

testCases.input: Array [9,8,7,6,5]; find left child of index 2
testCases.expected: Index 5 out of bounds, no child

approaches.name: Approach 3: Balanced Binary Search Tree
approaches.summary: Use a balanced BST (e.g., AVL tree or Red-Black tree) to store elements, enabling min and max extraction via in-order traversal endpoints. This approach provides efficient operations and can support additional queries like range searches.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n) for insert, deleteMin, and deleteMax

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement DEPQ using Balanced BST
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a double-ended priority queue using a balanced binary search tree with all required operations.
ladders.explanation: A double-ended priority queue (DEPQ) requires efficient insertion, deletion of minimum, and deletion of maximum elements. Using a balanced BST (like AVL or Red-Black tree) provides O(log n) time complexity for all operations by maintaining tree balance. The intuition is that a BST inherently organizes elements in sorted order, where the leftmost node is the minimum and the rightmost node is the maximum. By keeping the tree balanced, we ensure the height remains O(log n), making all traversal operations efficient. The complete solution involves implementing a balanced BST with standard insert and delete operations, plus specialized methods for extracting min and max. For insertion, we add a node while maintaining balance through rotations. For deleteMin, we find the leftmost node, remove it, and rebalance. For deleteMax, we find the rightmost node, remove it, and rebalance. The space complexity is O(n) to store n nodes. Connection to L1: This full problem integrates the key subroutine of finding and removing min/max nodes while maintaining balance. Common mistakes include forgetting to update heights/balances after rotations, not handling edge cases like empty trees, and incorrect rotation logic. Real code pattern: Define a Node class with value, left, right, height; implement insert(value), deleteMin(), deleteMax() methods with balancing.

examples.input: Insert: 5, 3, 7, 2, 4, 6, 8; deleteMin; deleteMax; deleteMin
examples.trace: 1. Insert 5: tree has root 5. 2. Insert 3: left child of 5. 3. Insert 7: right child of 5. 4. Insert 2: left child of 3. 5. Insert 4: right child of 3. 6. Insert 6: left child of 7. 7. Insert 8: right child of 7. Tree is balanced AVL. 8. deleteMin: find leftmost node 2, remove it, rebalance (no rotation needed). Tree now has min=3. 9. deleteMax: find rightmost node 8, remove it, rebalance (no rotation needed). Tree now has max=7. 10. deleteMin: find leftmost node 3, remove it, rebalance (rotation may occur if needed). Final tree has nodes 4,5,6 with min=4, max=6.

examples.input: Insert: 10, 20, 5; deleteMax; deleteMin; insert 15
examples.trace: 1. Insert 10: root 10. 2. Insert 20: right child of 10. 3. Insert 5: left child of 10. Tree balanced. 4. deleteMax: find rightmost node 20, remove it, rebalance (no rotation). Tree has nodes 5,10 with max=10. 5. deleteMin: find leftmost node 5, remove it, rebalance (no rotation). Tree has node 10 only. 6. insert 15: right child of 10. Tree has nodes 10,15 with min=10, max=15.

testCases.expected: Tree has nodes 3,4 with min=3, max=4
Tree has nodes 3,5,7,10,12 with min=3, max=12
Tree has nodes 4,6,7,8 with min=4, max=8
Tree has nodes 3,4 with min=3, max=4
Tree has nodes 50,75,100,125,150,200 with min=50, max=200
Tree has nodes 25,35 with min=25, max=35

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find and Remove Min/Max in Balanced BST
ladders.desc: Implement functions to find and remove the minimum or maximum node from a balanced BST while maintaining balance.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is crucial for DEPQ operations deleteMin and deleteMax. The intuition is that in a BST, the minimum value is the leftmost node (continuously follow left pointers until null), and the maximum is the rightmost node (follow right pointers). Removing these nodes requires careful handling to maintain BST properties and tree balance. For deleteMin: traverse left until finding a node with no left child, remove it, and if it has a right child, attach that child to the parent. Then update heights and perform rotations upward to rebalance. For deleteMax: similar but traverse right. The algorithm ensures O(log n) time due to traversal depth and rebalancing. Time complexity: O(log n) for traversal and rebalancing. Space: O(log n) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called within the full DEPQ implementation for min/max deletions. Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of BST traversal and removal. Common mistakes: not updating parent pointers correctly, forgetting to rebalance after deletion, and mishandling nodes with one child. Real code pattern: def deleteMin(node): if node.left is None: return node.right; node.left = deleteMin(node.left); update height; return balance(node).

examples.input: BST with root 10, left 5, right 15; left of 5 is 3; deleteMin
examples.output: Tree with root 10, left 5 (now left is null), right 15
examples.trace: 1. Start at root 10. 2. Go left to node 5. 3. Go left to node 3 (min). 4. Since 3 has no left child, remove it by returning its right child (null) to parent 5. 5. Update height of 5. 6. Balance 5 (no rotation needed). 7. Return to 10, update height, balance (no rotation). Final tree: 10 with left 5, right 15.

examples.input: BST with root 20, left 10, right 30; right of 30 is 40; deleteMax
examples.output: Tree with root 20, left 10, right 30 (now right is null)
examples.trace: 1. Start at root 20. 2. Go right to node 30. 3. Go right to node 40 (max). 4. Remove 40 by returning its left child (null) to parent 30. 5. Update height of 30. 6. Balance 30 (no rotation). 7. Return to 20, update height, balance. Final tree: 20 with left 10, right 30.

testCases.input: Tree: root 8, left 4, right 12; left of 4 is 2; deleteMin
Tree: root 8, left 4, right 12; right of 12 is 16; deleteMax
Tree: root 6, left 3, right 9; left of 3 is 1, right of 3 is 5; deleteMin
testCases.expected: Tree: root 6, left 3 (left null, right 5), right 9

testCases.input: Tree: root 6, left 3, right 9; left of 9 is 7, right of 9 is 11; deleteMax
testCases.expected: Tree: root 6, left 3, right 9 (left 7, right null)

testCases.input: Tree: root 50, left 30, right 70; left of 30 is 20, right of 30 is 40; deleteMin
testCases.expected: Tree: root 50, left 30 (left null, right 40), right 70

testCases.input: Tree: root 50, left 30, right 70; left of 70 is 60, right of 70 is 80; deleteMax
testCases.expected: Tree: root 50, left 30, right 70 (left 60, right null)

ladders.title: Core Logic — BST Traversal for Min and Max
ladders.desc: Understand how to find minimum and maximum elements in a binary search tree using traversal.
ladders.explanation: The core logic leverages the BST property: for any node, all values in the left subtree are smaller, and all in the right subtree are larger. Therefore, the minimum value is found by repeatedly moving to the left child until reaching a node with no left child—this is the leftmost node. Similarly, the maximum is the rightmost node found by moving to the right child until null. This works because the BST structure ensures sorted order when traversed in-order. The intuition is that the leftmost node has no smaller values in the tree, and the rightmost has no larger values. Algorithm: For min, start at root, while node.left is not null, set node = node.left, return node.value. For max, while node.right is not null, set node = node.right, return node.value. Time complexity: O(h) where h is tree height, O(log n) if balanced. Space: O(1) iterative. Connection to L1: This traversal is used in the sub-routine to locate min/max before removal. Connection to L3: It builds on the BST property. Common mistakes: assuming min/max are at root, not handling empty trees, and using recursion without base case. Real code pattern: def findMin(node): while node.left: node = node.left; return node.value.

examples.input: BST: root 10, left 5, right 15; left of 5 is 3
examples.trace: 1. Find min: start at 10, left to 5, left to 3, no left child, so min=3. 2. Find max: start at 10, right to 15, no right child, so max=15.

examples.input: BST: root 8, left 4, right 12; right of 12 is 16
examples.trace: 1. Find min: start at 8, left to 4, no left child, min=4. 2. Find max: start at 8, right to 12, right to 16, no right child, max=16.

testCases.input: Tree: root 15, left 10, right 25; left of 10 is 8
Tree: root 15, left 10, right 25; right of 25 is 30
Tree: root 50, left 30, right 70; left of 30 is 20, right of 30 is 40
Tree: root 50, left 30, right 70; left of 70 is 60, right of 70 is 80
Tree: root 100, left 50, right 150; left of 50 is 25, right of 50 is 75
Tree: root 100, left 50, right 150; left of 150 is 125, right of 150 is 175
Tree: root 9, left 5, right 12; left of 5 is 2, right of 5 is 7
Tree: root 9, left 5, right 12; left of 12 is 10, right of 12 is 14

ladders.title: Building Block — Balanced BST Insertion and Deletion
ladders.desc: Learn how to insert and delete nodes in a balanced BST (e.g., AVL tree) while maintaining height balance.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves maintaining a balanced BST after insertions and deletions to ensure O(log n) operations. In an AVL tree, balance is maintained by ensuring the height difference between left and right subtrees (balance factor) is at most 1. After insertion or deletion, we update heights and perform rotations (left, right, left-right, right-left) to restore balance. The intuition is that rotations reorganize the tree without violating BST properties, reducing height. For insertion: insert node as in BST, update heights from insertion point up, check balance factor, rotate if needed. For deletion: remove node as in BST (handling cases: no child, one child, two children), update heights upward, rotate to balance. Time complexity: O(log n) for traversal and rotations. Space: O(log n) for recursion. Connection to L2: Balanced operations enable efficient min/max traversal. Connection to L4: Builds on basic BST operations. Common mistakes: incorrect rotation cases, not updating heights after rotation, and forgetting to rebalance after deletion in two-child case. Real code pattern: def insert(node, value): if node is None: return Node(value); if value < node.value: node.left = insert(node.left, value); else: node.right = insert(node.right, value); update height; return balance(node).

examples.input: Insert 3 into empty AVL, then insert 5, then insert 4
examples.output: Balanced AVL with root 4, left 3, right 5
examples.trace: 1. Insert 3: tree has root 3. 2. Insert 5: right child of 3, balance factor of 3 is -1 (balanced). 3. Insert 4: insert as left child of 5. Update heights: 5 height=1, 3 height=2, balance factor of 3 is -2 (unbalanced right-heavy). Perform left rotation at 3? Wait, actually for AVL, after inserting 4 under 5, tree is 3 right 5 left 4. Balance factor at 3 is -2, at 5 is +1, so right-left rotation: first right rotate at 5, then left rotate at 3. Result: root 4 with left 3, right 5.

examples.input: AVL with root 10, left 5, right 15; delete 5
examples.output: Balanced AVL with root 10, left null, right 15
examples.trace: 1. Tree: 10 with left 5, right 15. 2. Delete 5: node 5 has no children, so remove it. Update height of 10: left subtree height becomes 0, right height 1, balance factor 1 (balanced). No rotation needed. Final tree: 10 with right 15.

testCases.input: AVL: root 5 left 3 right 7; left of 3 is 1; delete 3
AVL: root 5 left 3 right 7; right of 7 is 9; delete 7

ladders.title: Basic Operation — BST Insertion and Deletion
ladders.desc: Implement insertion and deletion in a standard binary search tree without balancing.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves adding or removing nodes in a BST while preserving the BST property: for any node, left subtree values are smaller, right subtree values are larger. For insertion: traverse from root, comparing values, and insert new node as a leaf at the appropriate null position. For deletion: three cases—node with no children (simply remove), node with one child (replace with child), node with two children (find inorder successor or predecessor, replace value, delete that successor/predecessor). The intuition is that BST structure enables efficient search by halving possibilities, but without balancing, operations can degrade to O(n) in worst-case skewed trees. Time complexity: O(h) where h is height, O(n) worst. Space: O(h) for recursion. Connection to L3: This is the foundation for balanced BST operations. Connection to L5: Builds on tree node structure. Common mistakes: not handling duplicate values (assume unique), incorrect pointer updates in deletion, and infinite recursion without base case. Real code pattern: def insert(node, value): if node is None: return Node(value); if value < node.value: node.left = insert(node.left, value); else: node.right = insert(node.right, value); return node.

examples.input: Insert 5 into empty BST, then insert 3, then insert 7
examples.trace: 1. Insert 5: create root node 5. 2. Insert 3: start at 5, 3 < 5, go left (null), insert as left child. 3. Insert 7: start at 5, 7 > 5, go right (null), insert as right child.

examples.input: BST: root 10, left 5, right 15; delete 5
examples.trace: 1. Tree: 10 with left 5, right 15. 2. Delete 5: node 5 has no children, so set parent 10's left to null. Result: 10 with right 15.

testCases.expected: Tree: root 30 left 10 or root 10 right 30 (inorder successor/predecessor)
Tree: root 50 left 20 right 40? Actually, inorder successor of 30 is 40, so replace 30 with 40 and delete 40 from right subtree.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Search Tree Structure and Properties
ladders.desc: Understand what a binary search tree is and its basic properties.
ladders.explanation: A binary search tree (BST) is a binary tree data structure where each node has a value, and for any node, all values in its left subtree are less than the node's value, and all values in its right subtree are greater. This property enables efficient search, insertion, and deletion operations. The intuition is that it organizes data in a sorted manner, allowing us to discard half of the tree at each step during search. A BST consists of nodes with fields: value, left child pointer, right child pointer. The root is the top node. Traversal methods include in-order (left, root, right) which yields sorted order. Key properties: uniqueness of values (typically), and height can vary from O(log n) in balanced trees to O(n) in skewed trees. Time complexity for basic operations is O(h) where h is height. Space: O(n) for storing nodes. Connection to L4: This foundation is necessary for implementing insertion and deletion. Common mistakes: confusing BST with binary tree, not maintaining the BST property during updates, and assuming balanced height. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, value): self.value = value; self.left = None; self.right = None.

examples.input: Create a BST with values 4,2,6
examples.trace: 1. Start with empty tree. 2. Insert 4 as root. 3. Insert 2: 2 < 4, so become left child of 4. 4. Insert 6: 6 > 4, so become right child of 4. Check property: left subtree has 2 (<4), right has 6 (>4).

examples.input: Check if tree with root 5, left 3, right 8 is a BST
examples.trace: 1. Root 5: left child 3 < 5, right child 8 > 5. 2. Check subtree: left subtree of 5 is just 3 (no children), ok. 3. Right subtree of 5 is just 8, ok. All nodes satisfy BST property.

testCases.input: Is a single node with value 1 a BST?
Tree: root 10 left 5 right 15; is it BST?
Tree: root 10 left 15 right 5; is it BST?
Tree: root 8 left 4 right 12; left of 4 is 2; is it BST?
Tree: root 8 left 4 right 12; left of 4 is 10; is it BST?
testCases.expected: No (10 > 4 but in left subtree of 8)

testCases.input: Tree: root 20 left 10 right 30; right of 10 is 25; is it BST?
Tree: root 20 left 10 right 30; right of 10 is 5; is it BST?
testCases.expected: No (5 < 10 but in right subtree of 10? Actually 5 < 10, but right child should be >10)

testCases.input: Insert 7,3,9 into BST; what is in-order traversal?
Insert 5,1,8 into BST; what is root value?
BST with root 6 left 2 right 9; what are left and right child values?
